title: Run your first program
instruction:
Here it is — your very first Go program. You don't need to change anything yet. Just hit Run and watch what happens.

Look at the output panel. Do you see "Hello, World!"? That's your computer following the instruction you gave it. One line of text, exactly as requested.
hint: No changes needed at all! Just press the Run button and watch the output appear.

title: Make it personal
instruction:
"Hello, World!" is the traditional first program every programmer writes. But let's make it yours.

Change the message so it says hello to you specifically. If your name is Jordan, make it print: Hello, Jordan!

The only thing you need to touch is the text between the quote marks.
hint: Find the text that says Hello, World! — replace World with your own name. Keep the exclamation mark and keep the quotes on both sides.

codeChecks.message: Replace 'World' with your own name — the output should say Hello, [your name]!

title: Print two lines
instruction:
Every fmt.Println call prints one line. Two calls means two lines of output.

The program below already prints the first line. Add a second fmt.Println below it that prints exactly this: Go is awesome!

Run it and watch both lines appear in order.
hint: Copy the existing fmt.Println line, paste it right below, and change the text inside the quotes to: Go is awesome!

title: Three lines — a short intro
instruction:
Now write a 3-line program that introduces you. Print these three things exactly:

Line 1: My name is [your name]
Line 2: I am learning Go
Line 3: Watch out, world!

Three separate fmt.Println calls. Three separate lines of output.
hint: You need three fmt.Println lines. The second and third lines must say exactly: I am learning Go and Watch out, world! — the first line can say anything with 'My name is' in it.

codeChecks.message: The first line should say My name is [your name]

title: Leave a comment
instruction:
A comment is a note you leave in your code. It starts with // and the computer ignores it completely — it's purely for humans reading the code.

This program has three Println calls. Replace each TODO line with a real comment explaining what that Println prints. The output should stay exactly the same — comments don't change what runs.
hint: Replace // TODO with a real comment like: // This prints the school name. Do this for all three Println calls.

codeChecks.message: Replace all three TODO lines with real comments — don't just leave them as TODO.

title: Find and fix the bugs
instruction:
This program has two bugs. It won't run until you fix both of them.

This is what programmers call debugging — finding and fixing mistakes. Every programmer does this every single day. Don't be discouraged when you see errors, they're just the computer telling you where to look.

Hint: Go is very picky about capital letters. And every opening quote mark needs a closing one.
hint: Bug 1: fmt.println should be fmt.Println — the P must be uppercase. Bug 2: the last Println is missing a closing quote mark before the closing parenthesis.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("The first line is fine")
	fmt.println("This line has a capital letter problem")
	fmt.Println("This line is missing a closing quote)
}
expectedOutput: This line has a capital letter problem
This line is missing a closing quote
